name: Orozco (3BLD)
description: A hybrid 3BLD method that combines M2 edge solving with 3-style corner commutators. Named after the method's popularizer, Orozco provides a middle ground between the simplicity of M2/OP and the speed of full 3-style. Edges are solved using M2-based techniques (with some 3-style edge commutators mixed in for common cases), while corners are solved using full 3-style commutators. This allows solvers to benefit from fast corner execution while maintaining the simpler M2 framework for edges during the transition to full 3-style.

steps.name: Memorization (Edges)
steps.description: Memorize edge cycles using letter tracing with the DF buffer (standard M2 buffer). Generate letter pairs for the memo sequence. The memo technique is the same as M2 — each letter represents a target for an M2 swap, though some common targets may be handled with 3-style commutators instead.
tips: DF buffer is standard for the M2 portion
Audio memo is popular for edges
Note which targets you plan to use 3-style for versus M2
Practice fast memorization — this is usually the bottleneck

steps.name: Memorization (Corners)
steps.description: Memorize corner cycles using letter tracing with the UBL or UFR buffer. Generate letter pairs, where each pair represents one 3-style corner commutator to execute. Visual memo with images is standard for corners.
tips: Same corner memo technique as full 3-style
Use letter pairs with vivid images for retention

steps.name: Execution (Corners - 3-Style)
steps.description: Execute corner 3-cycles using commutator algorithms, identical to the full 3-style method. Each letter pair in the corner memo corresponds to one commutator that cycles three corners. This gives fast corner execution comparable to world-class solvers.
tips: Learn corner commutators systematically by target position
Many corners share common interchange patterns
Corner execution is where Orozco shines over pure M2/OP
Practice until corner commutators are automatic

steps.name: Execution (Edges - M2 Hybrid)
steps.description: Execute edge swaps using primarily M2-based techniques, with 3-style commutators substituted for common or easy targets. The M2 framework provides a reliable backbone, while gradually incorporating commutators for specific targets improves speed. This hybrid approach allows incremental learning toward full 3-style edges.
tips: Start with pure M2 edges, then replace common targets with commutators
M-slice edge commutators are the easiest 3-style edges to learn first
Keep M2 as a fallback for any target where you have not learned the commutator
Gradually transition more targets to 3-style as you improve

pros: Faster than pure M2/OP due to 3-style corners
M2 edges are reliable and well-understood
Allows incremental improvement as more commutators are learned
Good competitive performance at intermediate BLD level
cons: Still slower than full 3-style for edges
Hybrid approach means two different execution styles to manage
M2 special cases still apply for edge targets
Eventually should be upgraded to full 3-style for top performance
